Parties: People's Bank v. Lokuge International Garments Ltd.
Bench: JAN de SILVA, CJ; Sripavan J; Ekanayake J
Keywords: prescription — estoppel — civil procedure code — preliminary issues — acknowledgment of debt — revival of action — letters of demand — burden of proof — admissibility of evidence — consent of parties — issues of law and fact
Summary: Facts This case revolves around an appeal from a judgment of the Commercial High Court concerning a financial transaction between People's Bank (the appellant) and Lokuge International Garments Ltd. (the respondent). The dispute arose from an export bill of exchange, where the appellant alleged that the respondent failed to pay certain sums owed. The High Court judge had decided to hear two specific issues as preliminary matters: whether the plaintiff's cause of action was prescribed under the Prescription Ordinance and whether the defendant was estopped from claiming any benefit from the plea of prescription due to prior admissions made in the pleadings. The appellant contended that the judge erred in treating these issues as preliminary, arguing that the action was revived by a letter from the defendant admitting liability. The case highlights the complexities of legal proceedings involving financial obligations and the implications of prescription laws. Issues The primary issues framed in this case are: Whether the plaintiff's cause of action is prescribed in law according to the provisions of the Prescription Ordinance? Whether the defendant is estopped from claiming any benefit on the plea of prescription due to prior admissions made in the pleadings? Applicable Law Statutes: Prescription Ordinance; Civil Procedure Code Cases Referred to: Pure Beverages Ltd. v. Shanil Fernando - [1997 (3) SLR 202]; Moorthiapillai v. Sivakaminathapillai - [14 NLR 30]; Bradford & Bingley plc v. Rashid - [2006] UKHL 37; Hanafi v. Nallamma - [1998 (1) SLR 73] Analysis Issue 1: Whether the plaintiff's cause of action is prescribed in law according to the provisions of the Prescription Ordinance? Rule applied: The Prescription Ordinance and the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court examined the nature of the preliminary issues raised by the parties, particularly focusing on the legal implications of prescription and the acknowledgment of debt. The learned High Court judge had framed the issues based on the consent of the parties, which is a critical aspect of the proceedings. The court noted that under Section 147 of the Civil Procedure Code, issues of law should be tried first if they can dispose of the case. However, the court emphasized that only pure questions of law should be considered as preliminary issues. The judgment referenced the case of Pure Beverages Ltd. v. Shanil Fernando, which established that an issue can only be tried as a preliminary issue if it is purely legal and the factual basis is not in dispute. The court also cited the opinion of De Z. Gunawardena, J., who stated that if an issue of law arises from a factual dispute, it should not be tried first. The court highlighted that the appellant's argument relied on a letter purportedly admitting liability, which was not adequately proven in the lower court. The court expressed concern that the existence of the letter was a matter of controversy and could not be resolved without addressing the factual issues surrounding it. Finding: The court concluded that the learned High Court judge erred in treating the issues as purely legal without considering the underlying factual disputes. Issue 2: Whether the defendant is estopped from claiming any benefit on the plea of prescription due to prior admissions made in the pleadings? Rule applied: The principles of estoppel and acknowledgment of debt. Court's reasoning: The court analyzed the implications of the defendant's alleged admissions in the pleadings and the effect of such admissions on the plea of prescription. The appellant argued that the action was revived by the defendant's acknowledgment of liability, which would reset the prescription period. The court referenced Moorthiapillai v. Sivakaminathapillai, where it was established that a written promise to pay or a payment on account can take a case out of the operation of the statute of limitations. The court also noted that the acknowledgment of debt must be clear and proven to have been made within the relevant time frame. The court emphasized that the burden of proof lies with the party asserting the acknowledgment, and in this case, the appellant failed to produce the necessary evidence to substantiate its claim. The court further distinguished between an acknowledgment of liability and a mere letter of demand, stating that the latter does not have the same legal effect in reviving a cause of action. Finding: The court found that the appellant did not adequately prove the existence of an acknowledgment that would revive the action. Interaction of Issues The interaction between the two issues is significant, as the determination of whether the plaintiff's cause of action is prescribed directly impacts the question of estoppel. If the court finds that the cause of action is indeed prescribed, then the defendant's plea of prescription would stand. Conversely, if the acknowledgment of debt is proven, it could negate the prescription claim. The court's analysis highlighted the interconnectedness of these issues, emphasizing the need for a comprehensive examination of both legal and factual elements before arriving at a conclusion. Conclusion The Supreme Court set aside the judgment of the learned High Court judge and directed that the case be tried on all issues, emphasizing the importance of addressing both legal and factual disputes comprehensively. The court made no order regarding costs, indicating a desire to allow the parties to resolve the matter on its merits without further financial burden. The judgment underscores the necessity for careful consideration of both legal principles and factual evidence in commercial disputes, particularly those involving financial obligations and the complexities of prescription laws. Keywords prescription — estoppel — civil procedure code — preliminary issues — acknowledgment of debt — revival of action — letters of demand — burden of proof — admissibility of evidence — consent of parties — issues of law and fact
Counsel: Rasika Dissanayake for Plaintiff-Appellant; Navin Marapana for Defendant-Respondent

Parties: R. A. Senanayake v. C. H. P. B/R No. 2660 and Others
Bench: Dr. Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; N. G. Amaratunga, J.; Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J.
Keywords: Ceiling on Housing Property Law — Jurisdiction — Divestment — Tenant Rights — Surplus Houses — Ministerial Approval — Legal Authority — Child's Rights — Equitable Considerations — Writ of Certiorari — Administrative Law — Housing Policy — Ownership — Housing Property Board of Review — Appeal Process
Summary: Facts The case revolves around the ownership and divestment of a housing property located at 43 2/2, Gregory's Road, Colombo 7, which was originally owned by R. A. Senanayake (the Appellant) and was occupied by C. H. P. B/R No. 2660 (the Respondent) as a tenant. The Appellant was married without children when the Ceiling on Housing Property Law No. 1 of 1973 came into effect, which limited the number of houses he could own to two. Consequently, he declared his intention to retain two houses and the remaining properties, including the one in question, were designated as surplus houses, vesting in the Commissioner for National Housing. The Appellant's contention is that he was entitled to divest the surplus house due to the birth of his child, Pascal, in 1977. The Commissioner for National Housing initially recommended the divestment of the house in question in 1985, which was later affirmed by the Ceiling on Housing Property Board of Review. However, the Respondent challenged this decision in the Court of Appeal, which quashed the orders made by the Commissioner, leading to the present appeal. The Court of Appeal found that the Commissioner acted without jurisdiction, as the Appellant had already divested another surplus house for the same child. The Supreme Court was tasked with determining whether the Court of Appeal had correctly interpreted the law and the facts surrounding the Appellant's application for divestment. Issues The primary issues before the Supreme Court included: (1) Whether the Court of Appeal failed to properly consider the law regarding the Appellant's application for divesting the premises. (2) Whether the Commissioner for National Housing had already made a decision to divest another house on account of the birth of the same child. (3) Whether the order to divest the premises in question was valid given the circumstances. Applicable Law Statutes: Ceiling on Housing Property Law No. 1 of 1973; Ceiling on Housing Property (Amendment) Law No. 34 of 1974. Cases Referred to: Gunawardene and Wijesooriya v. Minister of Local Government, Housing and Construction and Others [1999] 2 Sri LR 263. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the Court of Appeal failed to duly, properly and correctly consider the law in relation to the facts arising from the application of the Appellant for divesting of the premises? Rule applied: The Ceiling on Housing Property Law, particularly Sections 2, 8, 11, and 17A. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the provisions of the Ceiling on Housing Property Law, which aimed to regulate housing ownership and establish a framework for determining the permitted number of houses an individual could own. The Appellant's argument hinged on the interpretation of Section 11(3), which allowed for the divestment of surplus houses upon the birth of a child. The Court noted that the Appellant had already divested another house for the same child, which raised questions about the legality of further divestment for the same reason. The Court emphasized that the Commissioner acted beyond his jurisdiction by attempting to divest the house in question based on the same child, as the law did not permit multiple divestments for the same reason. The Court also highlighted the importance of adhering to the procedural requirements set forth in the law, including the necessity for ministerial approval and the proper application of the law's provisions. Finding: The Court concluded that the Court of Appeal had correctly interpreted the law and the facts, affirming its decision to quash the orders made by the Commissioner. Issue 2: Has the Commissioner for National Housing by Order A9 already made a decision to divest premises No. 43 2/1, Gregory's Road on account of the birth of a child to the Appellant? Rule applied: The provisions of the Ceiling on Housing Property Law regarding the divestment of surplus houses. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court analyzed the timeline of events leading to the divestment of premises No. 43 2/1, which had been executed under Section 11(3) of the law due to the birth of the Appellant's child. The Court noted that this divestment was valid and had been executed in accordance with the law. The Court emphasized that the Appellant could not claim further divestment for the same child, as the law only allowed for one house to be divested per child. Finding: The Court affirmed that the Commissioner had indeed made a prior decision to divest premises No. 43 2/1 for the same child, which precluded any further claims for divestment of additional houses on the same grounds. Issue 3: Is the Order A11 also divesting of the premises No. 43 2/2, Gregory's Road, in respect of the birth of the same child? Rule applied: The Ceiling on Housing Property Law, particularly Section 17A. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court scrutinized the order made by the Commissioner, which sought to divest the house in question based on the birth of the Appellant's child. The Court found that the Commissioner had exceeded his authority by attempting to divest the house for the same reason that had already been addressed with respect to another property. The Court reiterated that the law did not permit multiple divestments for the same child and that the Commissioner had acted ultra vires in making the order. Finding: The Court concluded that the order to divest premises No. 43 2/2 was invalid as it was made in excess of the Commissioner's authority. Interaction of Issues The issues presented were interrelated, as they all revolved around the interpretation of the Ceiling on Housing Property Law and the jurisdiction of the Commissioner for National Housing. The Court's findings on each issue reinforced the conclusion that the Commissioner had acted beyond his lawful authority, leading to the quashing of the orders made in favor of the Appellant. Conclusion The Supreme Court affirmed the decision of the Court of Appeal, concluding that the Appellant's appeal was without merit. The Court upheld the findings that the Commissioner had acted without jurisdiction in attempting to divest the house in question based on the birth of the same child for whom another house had already been divested. The appeal was refused, and the Respondent was awarded costs. Keywords Ceiling on Housing Property Law — Jurisdiction — Divestment — Tenant Rights — Surplus Houses — Ministerial Approval — Legal Authority — Child's Rights — Equitable Considerations — Writ of Certiorari — Administrative Law — Housing Policy — Ownership — Housing Property Board of Review — Appeal Process
Counsel: Romesh de Silva, P.C., Rohan Sahabandu, and Sugath Caldera for the 1st Respondent - Appellant. L. C. Seneviratne, P.C., and Kirthi Sri Gunawardena for the Petitioner-Respondent. Anil Gooneratne, Deputy Solicitor General for 2nd to 6th Respondent-Respondents.

Parties: Salley v. Colombo Municipal Council
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; N.G. Amaratunga, J; D.J. De S. Balapatabendi, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Hoardings — By-laws — Guidelines — Public interest litigation — Doctrine of public trust — Revenue enhancement — Transparency
Summary: Facts The petitioner, a former Deputy Mayor and rate-payer of the Colombo Municipal Council (CMC), filed this application in the public interest alleging that the 1st to 5th respondents failed to remove unauthorized advertising hoardings and granted approvals for new hoardings in violation of existing by-laws and guidelines. The petitioner contended that this failure, coupled with a lack of transparent revenue collection, constituted an infringement of the fundamental rights of the residents of the Colombo Municipality area under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. The CMC had previously established guidelines for hoardings in 2006, which were intended to regulate size, location, and safety. However, the 3rd respondent, the Municipal Commissioner, subsequently suspended these guidelines, claiming they required modification. The petitioner argued that this suspension was arbitrary and led to a proliferation of illegal hoardings, safety hazards, and significant loss of municipal revenue, as the Council failed to utilize competitive bidding processes similar to those employed by the Road Development Authority. The respondents raised a preliminary objection regarding the petitioner's locus standi, asserting that he failed to demonstrate a personal violation of his fundamental rights. Issues Whether the petitioner has the locus standi to maintain a fundamental rights application under Article 126(2) of the Constitution in the public interest. Whether the suspension of the advertisement guidelines by the 3rd respondent and the subsequent failure to enforce by-laws and guidelines constituted an infringement of the right to equality under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 3, 4(d), 12(1), 17, 28(d), 126 Municipal Councils Ordinance Cases Referred to: Bandhua Mukti Morcha v Union of India - [A.I.R. 1984 S.C. 802] Maharajah Singh v Uttara Pradesh - [A.I.R. 1976 S.C. 2602] S.P. Gupta v Union of India - [A.I.R. 1982 S.C. 149] Peoples Union for Democratic Rights v Union of India - [A.I.R. 1982 S.C. 1473] R v Inland Revenue Commissioners ex-parte National Federation of Self-Employment and Small Businesses Ltd. - [[1982] A.C. 617] R v Greater London Council ex-parte Blackburn - [[1976] 1 W.L.R. 550] R v Secretary of State for Foreign and Commonwealth Affairs Ex-parte Rees-Mogg - [[1994] Q.B. 552] Somawathie v Weerasinghe - [[1990] 2 Sri L.R. 121] Bulankulama v Secretary, Ministry of Industrial Development - [[2000] 3 Sri L.R. 243] Sriyani Silva v Chanaka Iddamalgoda - [[2003] 1 Sri L.R. 14] Yabbicom v King - [[1899] 1 Q.B. 444] E.P. Royappa v State of Tamil Nadu - [A.I.R. 1974 S.C. 555] Westminster Corporation v London & North Western Railway - [[1905] A.C. 426] Breen v Amalgamated Engineering Union - [[1971] 2 Q.B. 175] Padfield v Minister of Agriculture, Fisheries and Food - [[1968] A.C. 997] Saghir Ahmad v The State of Uttar Pradesh - [[1955] S.C.R. 707] Links Advertisers and Business Promoters v Commissioner, Corporation of the City of Bangalore - [A.I.R. 1977 S.C. 1646] Analysis Issue 1: Locus Standi Rule applied: Article 126(2) of the Constitution read with Articles 3, 4(d), and 17. Court's reasoning: The Court observed that while Article 126(2) traditionally requires a person whose rights are infringed to apply for relief, modern jurisprudence necessitates a broader, more expansive interpretation to make fundamental rights meaningful. Citing international and domestic precedents, the Court held that public interest litigation is a vital device to vindicate the rule of law. As a rate-payer and resident, the petitioner sought to protect public resources and ensure the enforcement of legal standards, which falls within the ambit of public interest. Finding: The preliminary objection on locus standi was overruled. Issue 2: Infringement of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution; the doctrine of public trust; principles of administrative law regarding the exercise of discretionary power. Court's reasoning: The Court found that the 3rd respondentâ€™s unilateral suspension of the guidelines was arbitrary and lacked legal authority. A public authority cannot waive or relax by-laws without specific statutory power. By failing to enforce existing by-laws and guidelines, the respondents acted in contravention of the doctrine of public trust, as they are guardians of public property. The lack of a transparent, competitive bidding process resulted in a significant loss of municipal revenue, which is an abuse of power and contrary to the rule of law. Finding: The 1st to 5th respondents infringed the fundamental rights of the petitioner and the residents under Article 12(1). Interaction of Issues The finding on locus standi allowed the Court to address the substantive merits of the administrative actions. The determination that the petitioner had standing enabled the Court to apply the doctrine of public trust to the respondents' conduct, reinforcing the conclusion that the arbitrary suspension of guidelines was not merely an administrative error but a breach of constitutional duty. Conclusion The application is allowed. The 1st respondent is directed to strictly enforce the 1989 by-laws and 2006 guidelines, remove all unauthorized hoardings, and formulate new, transparent guidelines incorporating competitive bidding by 01.01.2010. No order as to costs is made. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Hoardings — By-laws — Guidelines — Public interest litigation — Doctrine of public trust — Revenue enhancement — Transparency

Parties: Hapuarachchi and Others v. Dissanayake and Others
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; N.G. Amaratunga, J; Saleem Marsoof, J.
Keywords: Political party registration — Commissioner of Elections — Recognition of political party — Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Arbitrary decision — Reasons for decision — Procedural fairness — Parliamentary Elections Act
Summary: Facts The petitioners formed a political party known as Eksath Janatha Peramuna in 1999 and adopted a party constitution in 2000. The party intended to nominate candidates for various elections and maintained a membership exceeding 2200 individuals. On 31.10.2007, the 1st petitioner, acting as the General Secretary, applied to the 1st respondent Commissioner of Elections for the registration of the party as a recognized political party. This initial application was returned. A subsequent application was submitted on 14.12.2007. Following an inquiry held on 16.01.2008, the 1st respondent rejected the application by letter dated 21.01.2008 without providing any reasons for the decision. The petitioners alleged that the 1st respondent had registered five other political parties around the same time, despite those parties lacking the active political history of their own party. Consequently, the petitioners claimed that the rejection without reasons was arbitrary, unreasonable, and violated their fundamental rights under Articles 12(1), 12(2), and 14(1)(c) of the Constitution. Issues Whether the failure of the 1st respondent to provide reasons for the rejection of the application for registration as a recognized political party constituted an infringement of the petitioners' fundamental rights guaranteed under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 12(1), 12(2), 14(1)(c) Parliamentary Elections Act, No. 1 of 1981 Cases Referred to: Samalanka Ltd. v Weerakoon - [1994/v1-sri-lr-405] Karunadasa v Unique Gem Stones Ltd. - [1997/v1-sri-lr-256] Yaseen Omar v Pakistan International Airlines Corporation - [1999/v2-sri-lr-375] N.S.A.M. Nanayakkara v Peoples Bank - [sc-application-525/2002] Pure Spring Co. Ltd. v Minister of National Revenue - [1947/v1-d.l.r-501] Marta Stefan v General Medical Council - [1999/v1-w.l.r-1293] Minister of National Revenue v Wrights Canadian Ropes Ltd. - [1947/a.c-109] R v Gaming Board for Great Britain, Ex.p. Benaim and Khaida - [1970/v2-q.b-417] Mc Innes v Onslow Fane - [1978/v1-w.l.r-1520] R v Civil Service Appeal Board Ex.p. Cunningham - [1991/v4-all-e.r-310] R v Secretary of State for the Home Department Ex parte Doody - [1994/v1-a.c-531] Padfield v Minister of Agriculture Fisheries and Food - [1968/a.c-997] Lal Wimalasena v Asoka Silva - [sc-application-473/2003] Wijepala v Jayawardene - [sc-application-89/95] Manage v Kotakadeniya - [1997/v3-sri-lr-264] Suranganie Marapana v The Bank of Ceylon - [1997/v3-sri-lr-156] Osmond v Public Service Board of New South Wales - [1985/l.r.c-1041] E.P. Royappa v State of Tamil Nadu - [air-1974-sc-555] Re Poyser and Mills Arbitration - [1964/v2-q.b-467] R v Industrial Injuries Commissioner, Ex parte Howarth - [1968/v4-k.i.r-621] Analysis Issue 1: Failure to provide reasons Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution (Right to equality and equal protection of the law). Court's reasoning: The Court observed that while there was no general common law duty to provide reasons for administrative decisions, the legal landscape has evolved toward greater transparency and procedural fairness. The Court reasoned that the absence of reasons for a decision affecting a citizen's rights renders the decision-making process opaque and potentially arbitrary. By failing to provide reasons, the 1st respondent deprived the petitioners of the ability to understand the basis of the rejection or to effectively challenge it. The Court emphasized that equality is antithetic to arbitrariness, and withholding reasons for an administrative decision constitutes a denial of justice and a violation of the right to equal protection of the law. Finding: The decision of the 1st respondent to reject the application without providing reasons was arbitrary, unfair, and unreasonable, thereby violating the petitioners' fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Conclusion The court allowed the application and declared the decision of the 1st respondent dated 21.01.2008 null and void. The 1st respondent was directed to reconsider the petitioners' application and provide reasons for the decision following such reconsideration. Keywords Political party registration — Commissioner of Elections — Recognition of political party — Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Arbitrary decision — Reasons for decision — Procedural fairness — Parliamentary Elections Act

Parties: Gunaratne and Others v. Dissanayake and Others
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; N.G. Amaratunga, J; Saleem Marsoof, J.
Keywords: Political party registration — Recognition of political parties — Parliamentary Elections Act — Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Legitimate expectation — Reasons for decisions — Arbitrary decisions
Summary: Facts The petitioners, who were the founder, General Secretary, and an office bearer of the Sri Lanka Peratugami Pakshaya, sought to register their organization as a recognized political party. The party had been active since 1997 under various names, including X Group and X Party, engaging in political discourse through magazines, books, and public meetings. In January 2008, the petitioners applied to the 1st respondent, the Commissioner of Elections, for registration as a recognized political party. Following an inquiry on 17.01.2008, the 1st respondent rejected the application by letter dated 21.01.2008 without providing any reasons. The petitioners alleged that this refusal, while other new parties were simultaneously registered, violated their fundamental rights under Articles 12(1), 12(2), 14(1)(a), and 14(1)(c) of the Constitution. The respondents contended that the application was void ab initio for failure to submit a formal party constitution as required by Section 7(4)(b) of the Parliamentary Elections Act No. 1 of 1981, and that the party failed to meet the eligibility criteria under Section 7(5) of the same Act. Issues Whether the failure of the 1st respondent to provide reasons for the rejection of the application for registration as a political party constituted an infringement of the petitioners' fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the petitioners failed to comply with the mandatory requirements of Section 7(4)(b) of the Parliamentary Elections Act No. 1 of 1981 regarding the submission of a party constitution. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 3, 4, 12(1), 12(2), 14(1)(a), 14(1)(c) Parliamentary Elections Act No. 1 of 1981, Sections 7(4)(b), 7(5) Cases Referred to: Samalanka Ltd. v Weerakoon - [1994/v1-sri-lr-405] Karunadasa v Unique Gem Stones Ltd. - [1997/v1-sri-lr-256] Yaseen Omar v Pakistan International Airlines Corporation - [1999/v2-sri-lr-375] N.S.A.M. Nanayakkara v Peoples Bank - [sc-application-525/2002] Pure Spring Co. Ltd. v Minister of National Revenue - [1947/v1-dlr-501] Marta Stefan v General Medical Council - [1999/v1-wlr-1293] Minister of National Revenue v Wrights Canadian Ropes Ltd. - [1947-ac-109] R v Gaming Board for Great Britain, Ex.p. Benaim and Khaida - [1970/v2-qb-417] Mc Innes v Onslow Fane - [1978/v1-wlr-1520] R v Civil Service Appeal Board Ex.p. Cunningham - [1991/v4-all-er-310] R v Secretary of State for the Home Department Ex.p. Doody - [1994/v1-ac-531] Padfield v Minister of Agriculture Fisheries and Food - [1968-ac-997] Wijepala v Jayawardena - [sc-application-89/95] Lal Wimalasena v Asoka Silva - [sc-application-473/2003] Manage v Kotakadeniya - [1997/v3-sri-lr-264] Suranganie Marapana v The Bank of Ceylon - [1997/v3-sri-lr-156] Osmond v Public Service Board of New South Wales - [1985/lrc-const-1041] E.P. Royappa v State of Tamil Nadu - [air-1974-sc-555] Re Poyser and Mills Arbitration - [1964/v2-qb-467] R v Industrial Injuries Commissioner, Ex.p. Howarth - [1968/v4-kir-621] Analysis Issue 1: Failure to provide reasons Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution (Right to Equality) and the principles of procedural fairness. Court's reasoning: The Court observed a modern trend in administrative law requiring transparency. While there is no absolute common law duty to provide reasons, fairness dictates that when a decision affects rights, the failure to provide reasons may render the decision arbitrary. The Court found that the 1st respondent's failure to provide reasons in the rejection letter, coupled with the introduction of contradictory justifications in the affidavit, demonstrated an arbitrary exercise of power. Finding: The failure to provide reasons for the rejection constituted an infringement of the petitioners' fundamental rights under Article 12(1). Issue 2: Compliance with Section 7(4)(b) Rule applied: Section 7(4)(b) of the Parliamentary Elections Act No. 1 of 1981. Court's reasoning: The Court noted that the 1st respondent's affidavit did not cite the absence of a constitution as a reason for rejection, despite the Senior State Counsel's argument. The evidence showed that the petitioners had indeed submitted a constitution with their application. The respondent's reliance on the word "proposed" in the covering letter was insufficient to invalidate the application, especially since the 1st respondent's own contemporaneous records did not identify this as a deficiency. Finding: The petitioners had complied with the requirements of Section 7(4)(b). Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected because the lack of reasons (Issue 1) prevented the petitioners from knowing the true basis of the rejection, leading the respondents to advance inconsistent arguments before the Court (Issue 2). The Court's determination that the rejection was arbitrary was reinforced by the fact that the legal arguments raised by the State were not the actual reasons for the Commissioner's decision. Conclusion The decision of the 1st respondent to reject the application is declared null and void, and the petitioners' fundamental rights under Article 12(1) are held to have been infringed. The 1st respondent is directed to reconsider the application and provide reasons for his decision. No costs are ordered. Keywords Political party registration — Recognition of political parties — Parliamentary Elections Act — Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Legitimate expectation — Reasons for decisions — Arbitrary decisions

Parties: Sri Co-operative Industries Federation Limited v. Ajith Devapriya Kotalawala
Bench: Ms. Shiranee Tilakawardane, J.; R. A. N. G. Amaratunga, J.; Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J.
Keywords: Contract Law — Tender Document — Installation — Liability — Evidence — Hearsay Rule — Payment — Bill of Quantities — Electrical Installation — Consultant Engineer — Appeal — Supreme Court — Co-operative Societies Law — Recommendation — Burden of Proof
Summary: Facts The case revolves around a dispute between the Sri Co-operative Industries Federation Limited (the Appellant) and Ajith Devapriya Kotalawala (the Respondent) regarding the payment for electrical installation work performed at the Appellant's factory. The Appellant, a society registered under the Co-operative Societies Law No. 5 of 1972, had called for tenders in 1997 for the installation of an electrical system for its new factory. The Respondent submitted the lowest tender, which was accepted by the Appellant. The Bill of Quantities specified various items of work, including the installation of a 165 KVA transformer and the laying of cables. After the work was completed, the Appellant withheld payment for the transformer installation, claiming it was installed by the Lanka Electricity Company (LECO) and not by the Respondent. The Respondent filed a case in the District Court seeking payment for the withheld amounts, which the District Court initially ruled in favor of the Respondent for the transformer installation but denied the claim for cable laying due to insufficient evidence. The Appellant appealed this decision to the Court of Appeal, which upheld the District Court's ruling. The Supreme Court granted special leave to appeal on two questions of law regarding the sufficiency of evidence for the Respondent's claims and the Appellant's liability for the payment decreed by the lower courts. Issues Whether there is sufficient evidence for the Appellant to disclaim liability for the payment under item No: 01.01 of P4? Whether, in all the circumstances, the Appellant is not liable to pay the sum decreed by the District Court and the Court of Appeal? Applicable Law Statutes: Co-operative Societies Law No. 5 of 1972 Cases Referred to: Eliatamby v. Eliatamby - [27 NLR 396]; Sheila Seneviratne v. Shereen Dharmaratne - [1997] 1 Sri L.R. 76; Subramaniam v Public Prosecutor - [1956] 1 WLR 965; Powell and Wife v. Streatham Manor Nursing Home - [1935] AC 243; Munasinghe v. Vidanage - [69 NLR 97]; Attorney General v. Gnanapiragasam - [68 NLR 49]. Analysis Issue 1: Whether there is sufficient evidence for the Appellant to disclaim liability for the payment under item No: 01.01 of P4? Rule applied: The principle of contract law regarding the obligations of parties under a tender and the evidentiary burden of proof. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court analyzed the evidence presented, particularly focusing on the Bill of Quantities and the tender documents. The Court noted that the Respondent had claimed payment for the installation of the transformer, which he admitted was supplied and installed by LECO. The Court emphasized that the Respondent's own testimony indicated he did not perform the installation work as required under item 01.01 of the Bill of Quantities. The Court highlighted the importance of the Consultant Engineer's reports (P5 and P6), which recommended payment but were deemed hearsay since the Engineer was not called to testify. The Court concluded that the Respondent failed to prove he performed the work necessary to claim payment under item 01.01, as he did not install the transformer but merely connected it to the electrical room. Finding: The Supreme Court found that there was insufficient evidence to support the Respondent's claim for payment under item 01.01. Issue 2: Whether, in all the circumstances, the Appellant is not liable to pay the sum decreed by the District Court and the Court of Appeal? Rule applied: The burden of proof lies with the claimant to establish their case on a preponderance of probabilities. Court's reasoning: The Court reiterated that the Respondent had the burden to prove his entitlement to the claimed amounts. The evidence presented by the Respondent was insufficient to establish that he had completed the work as per the contract. The Court noted that the lower courts had relied heavily on the Engineer's letters without proper corroboration from the Engineer himself, which constituted a breach of the hearsay rule. The Court emphasized that the Respondent's claims were based on a misunderstanding of the contractual obligations and the nature of the work performed. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that the Appellant was not liable to pay the decreed amounts as the Respondent failed to substantiate his claims. Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated as the sufficiency of evidence directly impacts the Appellant's liability. If the Respondent cannot prove his claims, the Appellant cannot be held liable for the payments. The Court's analysis of the evidence and the contractual obligations clarified the relationship between the two issues. Conclusion The Supreme Court set aside the decisions of the lower courts regarding the payment for the transformer installation, allowing the appeal and dismissing the action filed by the Respondent in the District Court with costs. The Court emphasized the importance of adhering to the evidentiary standards and the contractual obligations outlined in the tender documents. Keywords Contract Law — Tender Document — Installation — Liability — Evidence — Hearsay Rule — Payment — Bill of Quantities — Electrical Installation — Consultant Engineer — Appeal — Supreme Court — Co-operative Societies Law — Recommendation — Burden of Proof
Counsel: Mr. T.M.S. Nanayakkara for Appellant; Mr. C. Ladduwahetty for Respondent

Parties: Francis Samarawickrema v. Dona Enatto Hilda Jayasinghe and Others
Bench: S. N. Silva, P.C., C.J.; Shiranee Tilakawardane, J.; Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J.
Keywords: fraud — due execution — Evidence Ordinance — Prevention of Frauds Ordinance — burden of proof — civil proceedings — attesting witnesses — notary public — rei vindicatio
Summary: Facts The case revolves around a dispute over the title of a land parcel known as 'Pokunuwattekattiya' in Kalutara North, which was originally owned by the Respondents and their deceased mother. The Appellant, Francis Samarawickrema, claimed to have purchased the land through a deed executed on 24th March 1976, but the Respondents contended that the deed was fraudulently executed. The initial trial in the District Court ruled in favor of the Appellant, but this decision was overturned by the Court of Appeal, which found that the Appellant had failed to prove the due execution of the deed. The Supreme Court was approached for special leave to appeal against this ruling, focusing on two main questions: whether the Court of Appeal erred in its finding of fraud and whether it failed to consider the evidence of the notary public under the Evidence Ordinance. Issues The key issues framed for determination were: Whether the Court of Appeal erred in holding that Deed No. 4880 was fraudulently executed when the required standard of proof for fraud was not met? Whether the Court of Appeal erred in law by not considering the evidence of the notary public in terms of Section 33 of the Evidence Ordinance? Applicable Law Statutes: Prevention of Frauds Ordinance No. 7 of 1840; Evidence Ordinance No. 14 of 1895. Cases Referred to: Hilda Jayasinghe v. Francis Samarawickrema [1982] 1 Sri LR 249; Kirihanda v. Ukkuwa [1892] 1 S.C.R. 216; Somanather v. Sinnetamby [1899] 1 Tambiah 38; Seneviratne v. Mendis 6 C.W.R. 211; Baronchy Appu v. Poidohamy 2 Browns’ Reports 221; Fernando v. Fernando 6 Ceylon Weekly Reporter 262; Lakshmanan Chettiar v. Muttiah Chettiar 50 NLR 337; Yoosoof v. Rajaratnam 74 NLR 9; Associated Battery Manufacturers (Ceylon) Ltd. v. United Engineering Workers Union 77 NLR 541; Caledonian Estate Ltd. v. Hilaman 79 - 1 NLR 421. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the Court of Appeal erred in holding that Deed No. 4880 was fraudulently executed? Rule applied: The standard of proof for fraud in civil cases. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the evidence presented in the case, particularly focusing on the execution of Deed No. 4880. The Court noted that the Respondents alleged that their signatures were obtained on blank papers, which were later filled in to create the deed. The Court emphasized that the burden of proof lies with the party alleging fraud, and in this case, the Respondents had to demonstrate that the deed was executed fraudulently. The Supreme Court highlighted that the Court of Appeal failed to apply the correct standard of proof required to establish fraud, which is higher than the ordinary civil standard. The Court also pointed out that the Respondents did not provide sufficient evidence to support their claims of fraud, and the mere assertion of fraud was not enough to overturn the findings of the District Court. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that the Court of Appeal erred in its finding of fraud, as the evidence did not meet the required standard. Issue 2: Whether the Court of Appeal erred in law by not considering the evidence of the notary public? Rule applied: Section 33 of the Evidence Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court analyzed the implications of the notary's death and the subsequent application to adopt his previous testimony under Section 33 of the Evidence Ordinance. The Court noted that the District Court had refused to admit the notary's evidence from the first trial, which was a significant oversight. The Supreme Court emphasized that the notary's testimony was crucial in establishing the due execution of the deeds, and the failure to consider this evidence constituted a legal error. The Court reiterated that the notary's evidence should have been included in the assessment of the case, especially given the allegations of fraud against him. Finding: The Supreme Court determined that the Court of Appeal's failure to consider the notary's evidence was a significant error that affected the outcome of the case. Interaction of Issues The two issues are interrelated, as the determination of whether the deed was fraudulently executed hinges on the evidence of due execution, which includes the notary's testimony. The Court of Appeal's failure to consider the notary's evidence directly impacted its finding of fraud, demonstrating the interconnectedness of the issues at hand. Conclusion The Supreme Court allowed the appeal, vacated the judgment of the Court of Appeal, and affirmed the judgment of the District Court, which had ruled in favor of the Appellant. The Court emphasized the importance of adhering to the correct standards of proof and the necessity of considering all relevant evidence in determining the validity of the deeds in question. Keywords fraud — due execution — Evidence Ordinance — Prevention of Frauds Ordinance — burden of proof — civil proceedings — attesting witnesses — notary public — rei vindicatio
Counsel: Mr. S. C. B. Walgampaya, P.C., and Mr. L.K.J. Perera for Appellant. Ms. Maureen Seneviratne, P.C., and Mr. S.A.D.S. Suraweera for Respondents

Parties: International Cement Traders (PVT) Ltd. v. Bandaranaike and Others
Bench: S.N. Silva, J.; K. Sripavan, J.; P. Ratnayaka, J
Keywords: Delegated legislation — Ultra vires — Arbitrary and capricious decisions — Revenue Protection Act — Import duties — Customs Ordinance — Free Trade Agreements — Discretion of the Minister — Judicial review — Excess duty payment — Section 18 of the Customs Ordinance
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, International Cement Traders (Pvt) Ltd., is a company engaged in the importation of Portland cement from Malaysia. On 23rd June 2000, the Minister of Finance and Planning issued a Revenue Protection Order, published in Gazette Extraordinary No. 1137/36, which imposed a preferential import duty of 9.5% on cement of Indian origin and increased the import duty on cement from other countries to 25%. The Petitioner challenged this order, alleging it was ultra vires, arbitrary, and discriminatory, and sought a Writ of Certiorari to quash the order and a Writ of Mandamus to compel the Director General of Customs to refund excess duties paid. The Respondents contended that the duty adjustment was necessary to implement a bilateral Free Trade Agreement with India. The Court of Appeal dismissed the Petitioner's application on 25th March 2003. The Petitioner subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court, which granted leave to appeal on several questions of law regarding the validity of the delegated legislation and the Petitioner's entitlement to a refund. Issues Whether the Revenue Protection Order published in Gazette Extraordinary No. 1137/36 constitutes valid delegated legislation. Whether the Minister of Finance acted ultra vires or arbitrarily under Section 2 of the Revenue Protection Act No. 19 in issuing the order. Whether the Petitioner is entitled to a Writ of Mandamus for a refund of excess duty without first exhausting the statutory procedure under Section 18 of the Customs Ordinance. Applicable Law Statutes: Revenue Protection Act No. 19 Customs Ordinance Cases Referred to: Ashwathanarayana Setty v. State of Karnataka - [1989-sup-1-scc-696] [sc-special-determination-17/97] Analysis Issue 1: Validity of delegated legislation Rule applied: The principle that delegated legislation is valid provided it conforms to the power granted by the enabling Act. Court's reasoning: The Court noted that the Revenue Protection Order was subsequently approved by Parliament via a resolution under Section 10 of the Customs Ordinance and published in Gazette Extraordinary No. 1156/6. The Court clarified that the Gazette notification marked P12 was irrelevant, as the proper legal basis for the approval was the resolution published in the aforementioned Gazette. Finding: The notification constitutes valid delegated legislation that was duly approved by Parliament. Issue 2: Ultra vires and arbitrary exercise of power Rule applied: The doctrine of wide latitude for the State in matters of economic and fiscal regulation. Court's reasoning: The Court held that the State possesses broad discretion to select objects, persons, or countries for taxation to achieve social and economic policies. As no mala fides were pleaded against the Minister, the Court declined to interfere with the discretionary fiscal decision, noting that the State may differentiate between countries to fulfill international obligations like the Free Trade Agreement. Finding: The order was not ultra vires or arbitrary. Issue 3: Entitlement to a refund Rule applied: Section 18 of the Customs Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The Court emphasized that mandamus is an extraordinary remedy not granted at the Petitioner's convenience. Because the Petitioner failed to utilize the specific statutory mechanism provided under Section 18 of the Customs Ordinance for claiming refunds of excess duty, the Court held that the request for mandamus was procedurally improper. Finding: The Petitioner is not entitled to a Writ of Mandamus as the statutory remedy was not exhausted. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected; the determination that the fiscal policy was a valid exercise of state discretion (Issue 2) and that the legislation was properly approved by Parliament (Issue 1) confirms the legality of the duty collection. Consequently, because the collection was legal and the Petitioner failed to follow the mandatory statutory procedure for refunds (Issue 3), the Petitioner's claim for relief fails in its entirety. Conclusion The judgment of the Court of Appeal is affirmed. The appeal is dismissed without costs. Keywords Delegated legislation — Ultra vires — Arbitrary and capricious decisions — Revenue Protection Act — Import duties — Customs Ordinance — Free Trade Agreements — Discretion of the Minister — Judicial review — Excess duty payment — Section 18 of the Customs Ordinance

Parties: Chandrasena v. Buddhika and Others
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; D.J. De S. Balapatabendi, J.; K. Sripavan, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Article 11 of the Constitution — Freedom from torture — Assault by Police officers — Medical evidence — Liability of the State — Administrative action — Police investigation
Summary: Facts The petitioner, Uduwa Athukoralage Chandrasena, alleged that he was arrested on 27.06.2007 by police officers while on his way to a funeral. He claimed that the 1st respondent and other unknown officers assaulted him with clubs near a volleyball court after inquiring about illicit liquor. The petitioner stated that he was subsequently taken to the Baduraliya Police Station, held in a cell, and released on police bail later that evening. He further alleged that the police threatened him against seeking medical treatment. On 29.06.2007, the petitioner appeared before the Magistrates Court of Matugama, where he pleaded not guilty to charges of possessing illicit liquor and informed the Magistrate of the assault. Due to persistent pain, he was admitted to the Kalutara-North Hospital on 04.07.2007 and was later examined by a Consultant Judicial Medical Officer. The 2nd respondent, the Officer-in-Charge of the Baduraliya Police Station, provided evidence that he was on leave during the incident. The 1st respondent contended that he was engaged in other police duties at the station at the time of the arrest and provided documentary evidence to support his alibi. Issues Whether the 1st and 2nd respondents were responsible for the infringement of the petitioner's fundamental rights under Article 11 of the Constitution. Whether the petitioner's fundamental right to freedom from torture guaranteed by Article 11 of the Constitution was infringed by executive or administrative action. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 11 Code of Criminal Procedure Act No. 15 of 1979 Cases Referred to: Kapugeekiyana v. Hettiarachchi - [1984/v2-sri-lr-153] Velmurugu v. A.G. - [Fundamental Rights Vol. I 196] Loveden v. Loveden - [(1810) 2 Hagg. Con. 1.3] Pita Kandalage Gamini Jayasinghe v. P.C. Samarawickrama - [sc-appeal-157/91] Amal Sudath Silva v. Kodituwakku - [1987/v2-sri-lr-119] Ratnasiri v. Devasurendran - [1994/v3-sri-lr-127] Analysis Issue 1: Responsibility of the 1st and 2nd respondents Rule applied: The burden of proof in fundamental rights applications requires the petitioner to satisfy the court on a balance of probabilities regarding the identity of the perpetrators. Court's reasoning: The 2nd respondent provided documentary evidence (out and in entries) proving he was on leave during the incident. Regarding the 1st respondent, the court examined station records (MOIB) which placed him at the police station performing other duties at the time of the arrest. The petitioner failed to provide independent evidence to substantiate his claim that the 1st respondent was present at the scene. Finding: The court held that neither the 1st nor the 2nd respondent was involved in the arrest or the assault. Issue 2: Infringement of Article 11 Rule applied: Article 11 of the Constitution (freedom from torture) and the principle that medical evidence is sufficient to prove torture when a petitioner is in state custody. Court's reasoning: While the specific officers were not identified, the Medico-Legal Report confirmed the petitioner sustained injuries consistent with repeated assault by blunt weapons. The court applied the principle that when a person in state custody develops injuries not present at the time of arrest, the burden shifts to the state to explain them. The state failed to provide such an explanation. Finding: The court held that the petitioner's fundamental rights under Article 11 were violated by executive and/or administrative action. Interaction of Issues The failure to identify the specific individual officers did not preclude a finding of a fundamental rights violation. The court determined that while the petitioner could not prove the specific involvement of the 1st and 2nd respondents, the medical evidence and the fact that the assault occurred under the color of state office established state liability for the infringement of Article 11. Conclusion The court declared that the petitioner's fundamental rights under Article 11 of the Constitution were infringed by executive and/or administrative action. The State was directed to pay the petitioner Rs. 75,000 as compensation and costs within three months. Keywords Fundamental rights — Article 11 of the Constitution — Freedom from torture — Assault by Police officers — Medical evidence — Liability of the State — Administrative action — Police investigation

Parties: S. Sivayanama and S. Sivasivaya v. People's Bank and Others
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; N.G. Amaratunga, J.; Saleem Marsoof, J.
Keywords: natural justice — jurisdiction — appeal — Finance Act — declaratory relief — fair hearing — preliminary objection — possession — ownership — vesting order — State Mortgage and Investment Bank Act — Civil Procedure Code — administrative law — rights of parties
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal by S. Sivayanama and S. Sivasivaya, who are the owners of a property located at No. 300, Main Street, Matale. The property was inherited from their father, who had owned and occupied it for over 30 years. The appellants received notices from the People's Bank, the first defendant-respondent, claiming that the property had vested in the bank under the Finance Act, No. 11 of 1963, following a gazette notification dated 11 July 1979. The appellants contested this claim by filing action No. 4349/L in the District Court of Matale, seeking a declaration that the property was not liable to be acquired and an injunction against eviction. The bank countered by asserting that the property had indeed vested in it, citing the relevant provisions of the Finance Act. During the proceedings, the bank raised a preliminary objection regarding the jurisdiction of the District Court to hear the case, which was upheld by the learned Additional District Judge, leading to the dismissal of the appellants' action. The appellants subsequently appealed to the Court of Appeal, which initially heard arguments on the preliminary objection but ultimately dismissed the appeal on its merits without allowing the appellants to present their case fully. This led to the appellants seeking special leave to appeal to the Supreme Court on the grounds that their right to a fair hearing was violated. Issues Whether the judgment of the Court of Appeal, which decided the appeal on its merits without allowing the appellants an opportunity to be heard, violated the principles of natural justice? Whether the Court of Appeal erred in treating the order of the District Court as a final order when it was merely interlocutory? Applicable Law Statutes: Finance Act, No. 11 of 1963; State Mortgage and Investment Bank Act; Civil Procedure Code. Cases Referred to: Ranasinghe v. Ceylon State Mortgage Bank - [1981] 1 Sri L.R. 121; Siriwardena v. Air Ceylon Ltd. - [1984] 1 Sri L.R. 286; White v. Brunton - [1984] 2 All E.R. 606; State Graphite Corporation v. Fernando - [1982] 2 Sri L.R. 590; Board of Education v. Rice - [1911] A.C. 179. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the judgment of the Court of Appeal, which decided the appeal on its merits without allowing the appellants an opportunity to be heard, violated the principles of natural justice? Rule applied: The principle of natural justice, particularly the right to a fair hearing (audi alteram partem). Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court, led by Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J., emphasized the fundamental principle of natural justice, which mandates that no party should be condemned unheard. The Court noted that the appellants were not given a fair opportunity to present their case on the merits of their appeal, as the Court of Appeal had only heard arguments on the preliminary objection raised by the bank. The judgment of the Court of Appeal was criticized for proceeding to decide the appeal on its merits without affording the appellants the chance to respond, which is a violation of the principles of natural justice. The Court highlighted that the right to be heard is a universal application in legal proceedings, and the failure to provide such an opportunity undermines the integrity of the judicial process. The Court referenced various legal precedents, including the case of Ridge v. Baldwin, which underscores the necessity of a fair hearing in administrative law contexts. The Court concluded that the Court of Appeal's decision was flawed as it did not adhere to the established norms of fair hearing, thus rendering the judgment invalid. Finding: The Supreme Court found in favor of the appellants, concluding that their right to a fair hearing was indeed violated. The appeal was allowed, and the judgment of the Court of Appeal was set aside. Issue 2: Whether the Court of Appeal erred in treating the order of the District Court as a final order when it was merely interlocutory? Rule applied: The distinction between final and interlocutory orders in the context of appeals. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the nature of the order made by the District Court, which dismissed the appellants' action based on a preliminary objection regarding jurisdiction. The Court noted that the order was not a final determination of the rights of the parties but rather an interlocutory order that should have been addressed separately before proceeding to the merits of the case. The Court of Appeal's failure to recognize this distinction and its decision to dismiss the appeal on the merits without addressing the preliminary objection first was deemed a procedural error. The Court reiterated that a proper legal framework requires that preliminary objections be resolved before delving into substantive issues, as this ensures that the parties' rights are adequately protected. The Court cited relevant legal principles and cases to support its position, emphasizing the importance of adhering to procedural norms in judicial proceedings. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that the Court of Appeal had indeed erred in treating the District Court's order as final, reinforcing the need for a structured approach to appeals. Interaction of Issues The issues of natural justice and the classification of the District Court's order are interrelated, as the failure to provide a fair hearing directly impacted the Court of Appeal's ability to make a sound judgment regarding the nature of the order. The lack of opportunity for the appellants to present their case on the merits not only violated their rights but also led to a mischaracterization of the order as final rather than interlocutory. Conclusion The Supreme Court allowed the appeal, set aside the judgment of the Court of Appeal, and directed that the case be heard de novo, ensuring that the appellants would have the opportunity to present their case fully. The Court emphasized the importance of adhering to principles of natural justice and procedural fairness in judicial proceedings, reiterating that a fair hearing is a cornerstone of the legal system. The Court also noted that the respondents could raise preliminary objections in the Court of Appeal if they so desired. There was no order as to costs.
Counsel: A.R. Surendran, PC, with K.V.S. Ganesharajan, Nadarajah Kandeepan and Ms. Dharshini for Plaintiffs-Appellants-Appellants; Manohara de Silva, PC, with D. Weeraratne for 1st Defendant-Respondent-Respondent

Parties: Upali Dharmasiri Welaratne v. Wesley Jayaraj Moses
Bench: Dr. Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J.; D. J. de S. Balapatabandi, J.
Keywords: contempt of court — interim injunction — undertaking — civil contempt — burden of proof — admissibility of evidence — photographic evidence — jurisdiction of court — violation of court order — standard of proof — disobedience of court orders — legal principles — appellate jurisdiction — procedural irregularity — rights of parties — judicial discretion
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal against a conviction for contempt of court, where the Appellant, Upali Dharmasiri Welaratne, was found guilty of violating an undertaking given to the Court of Appeal. This undertaking was made during a hearing concerning an interim injunction sought by the Respondent, Wesley Jayaraj Moses, to prevent further construction on a property adjacent to his own. The Respondent had filed a revision application challenging the District Court's refusal to grant an interim injunction to restrain the Appellant from destroying structures on his property. The Court of Appeal had issued an interim injunction based on the Appellant's undertaking to maintain the status quo, which he later violated by continuing construction activities. The Appellant was sentenced to two years of rigorous imprisonment and a fine of Rs. 500,000, with an additional six months of imprisonment for non-payment. The Appellant contended that the injunction was irregular as it was issued without hearing both parties fully. Issues Whether the violation of an irregular order amounts to contempt of court? Whether the Court of Appeal erred in admitting photographic evidence? Whether the Respondent discharged the burden of proof in establishing contempt? Whether the Court of Appeal considered extraneous factors in convicting the Appellant? Applicable Law Statutes: Article 105 of the Constitution of Sri Lanka; Evidence Ordinance No. 14 of 1895; Evidence (Special Provisions) Act No. 14 of 1995. Cases Referred to: Silva v. Appuhamy - [4 NLR 178]; de Alwis v. Rajakaruna - [68 NLR 180]; Hussain v. Hussain - [1986] 1 All ER 961; York Timbers Ltd v. Minister of Water Affairs & Forestry - [2003 (4) SALR 477]; Sri Lanka Ports Authority and Another v. Jugolinija-Boal East - [1981] 1 Sri LR 18; Kularatne and Another v. Rajapakse - [1985] 1 Sri LR 24; Shahul Hameed and Another v. Ranasinghe and Others - [1990] 1 Sri LR 104; Peiris and Another v. Perera and Another - [2002] 1 Sri LR 128. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the violation of an irregular order amounts to contempt of court? Rule applied: The principle that an injunction granted by a competent court must be obeyed until discharged, regardless of procedural irregularities. Court's reasoning: The Court emphasized that the Appellant's undertaking was equivalent to a court order, thus making its breach contemptuous. The Court of Appeal had jurisdiction to issue the injunction, and the Appellant's argument regarding the irregularity was dismissed as he voluntarily submitted to the court's jurisdiction by giving the undertaking. The Court cited precedents affirming that an undertaking to the court is as binding as a court order. The Appellant's claim that the injunction was issued without a proper hearing was undermined by the fact that he had given an undertaking in open court, which negated the need for further inquiry. Finding: The Court concluded that the violation of the undertaking constituted contempt of court. Issue 2: Whether the Court of Appeal erred in admitting photographic evidence? Rule applied: The admissibility of photographic evidence under the Evidence Ordinance and the Evidence (Special Provisions) Act. Court's reasoning: The Court found that the photographs were admissible as they were relevant to proving the Appellant's contempt. The Appellant's objections regarding the manipulation of dates on the photographs were dismissed as he failed to raise these objections in a timely manner during the contempt inquiry. The Court noted that the Respondent had provided sufficient evidence to establish the authenticity of the photographs. Finding: The Court upheld the admission of the photographic evidence as valid and relevant. Issue 3: Whether the Respondent discharged the burden of proof in establishing contempt? Rule applied: The standard of proof required for civil contempt is beyond a reasonable doubt. Court's reasoning: The Court analyzed the evidence presented by both parties and concluded that the Respondent had successfully established that the Appellant had violated the undertaking. The Court noted that the Appellant's testimony was inconsistent and unconvincing, while the Respondent's evidence was corroborated by witnesses and photographic evidence. The Court emphasized that the Appellant had knowledge of the undertaking and the ability to comply with it, yet willfully disobeyed it. Finding: The Court determined that the Respondent met the burden of proof required for a finding of contempt. Issue 4: Whether the Court of Appeal considered extraneous factors in convicting the Appellant? Rule applied: The principle that courts must base their decisions solely on the evidence presented. Court's reasoning: The Court found no merit in the Appellant's claim that the Court of Appeal had considered extraneous factors. The reference to the Appellant's profession as an attorney-at-law was deemed irrelevant to the conviction. The Court maintained that the decision was based on the evidence of contempt and the Appellant's conduct. Finding: The Court concluded that the Appellant's claim regarding extraneous factors was unfounded. Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated as they all stem from the Appellant's violation of the court's undertaking and the subsequent proceedings to establish contempt. The determination of whether the injunction was irregular directly impacts the validity of the contempt charge, while the admissibility of evidence and the burden of proof are crucial in assessing the overall findings of contempt. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the appeal, affirming the Court of Appeal's conviction and sentence for contempt of court, emphasizing the binding nature of undertakings given to the court and the importance of adhering to court orders. The Court found no grounds to interfere with the sentence imposed, considering the Appellant's age and the nature of the contempt. The appeal was dismissed without costs. Keywords: contempt of court — interim injunction — undertaking — civil contempt — burden of proof — admissibility of evidence — photographic evidence — jurisdiction of court — violation of court order — standard of proof — disobedience of court orders — legal principles — appellate jurisdiction — procedural irregularity — rights of parties — judicial discretion
Counsel: L. C. Seneviratne, P.C. for the Defendant-Respondent-Respondent-Appellant. Ransiri Fernando with Sarath Walgamage for the Plaintiff-Petitioner-Petitioner-Respondent.

Parties: Ranaweerage Sarath Nandalal and Ariyachakra Bandaranayake v. Public Enterprise Reform Commission and Others
Bench: Justice Vijith K. Malalgoda, P.C.; Justice A.H.M.D. Nawaz; Justice Janak De Silva
Keywords: fundamental rights — public interest — administrative actions — share sale — quasi contract — legal ownership — Treasury Bonds — corporate governance — auditor removal — profit entitlement
Summary: Facts The case revolves around two applications filed under Article 126 of the Constitution, challenging the sale of 90% of the shares of the Sri Lanka Insurance Corporation Ltd. (SLICL), a government-owned entity, to private sector companies. The petitioners, Ranaweerage Sarath Nandalal and Ariyachakra Bandaranayake, argued that the sale was conducted unlawfully and infringed upon their fundamental rights as citizens. The respondents included various private companies and government officials involved in the transaction. The sale was executed under a Share Sale and Purchase Agreement (SSPA), which the petitioners contended was invalid from the outset. The Supreme Court, in its judgment delivered on 4 June 2009, found that the petitioners had established an infringement of their rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution due to wrongful executive actions. The Court declared the SSPA null and void ab initio, thereby invalidating the sale of shares. The judgment mandated that the shares be returned to the Secretary to the Treasury, and the Board of Directors of SLICL be removed. The Court also ordered the government to return the purchase price of Rs. 6.05 billion to the purchasing companies, along with profits accrued during the period of ownership. Issues The primary issues framed in this case include: Whether the sale of 90% of the shares of SLICL infringed upon the fundamental rights of the petitioners? Whether the SSPA was valid or void ab initio? Whether the petitioners are entitled to any relief for the alleged infringement of their rights? Applicable Law Statutes: Article 12(1) of the Constitution of Sri Lanka. Cases Referred to: Ganeshanathan v. Vivienne Goonewardene [(1984) 1 Sri. L. R. 319]; Jeyaraj Fernandopulle v. Premachandra De Silva and Others [(1996) 1 Sri. L. R. 70]. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the sale of 90% of the shares of SLICL infringed upon the fundamental rights of the petitioners? Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution of Sri Lanka. Court's reasoning: The Court began its analysis by emphasizing the significance of Article 12(1), which guarantees equality before the law and equal protection of the law. The petitioners argued that the sale of shares was conducted without proper legislative authority and without adequate public consultation, thereby infringing their rights as citizens. The Court noted that the SLICL was a fully government-owned entity, and any decision regarding its privatization must adhere to constitutional and legal standards. The Court highlighted that the executive actions taken to facilitate the sale were not only arbitrary but also lacked transparency, which is essential in matters involving public assets. The judgment referenced previous cases where the Court had upheld the importance of public interest and the need for accountability in government actions. The Court concluded that the petitioners had indeed established a violation of their fundamental rights due to the unlawful sale of shares. Finding: The Court found in favor of the petitioners, declaring that their fundamental rights had been infringed. Issue 2: Whether the SSPA was valid or void ab initio? Rule applied: Principles of contract law and public interest. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the SSPA in detail, noting that it was executed without proper legislative backing and in contravention of established procedures for the privatization of state assets. The Court emphasized that any agreement involving public assets must be subjected to rigorous scrutiny to ensure compliance with the law. The judgment pointed out that the SSPA lacked the necessary approvals and was executed under circumstances that raised serious questions about its legitimacy. The Court referred to the principle that contracts entered into without lawful authority are void ab initio, meaning they have no legal effect from the outset. The Court's analysis underscored the importance of maintaining public trust in government transactions, particularly those involving significant public assets. Finding: The Court held that the SSPA was null and void ab initio, thereby invalidating the sale of shares. Interaction of Issues The issues of fundamental rights infringement and the validity of the SSPA are intricately linked. The Court's finding that the sale infringed upon the petitioners' rights was largely predicated on the determination that the SSPA was invalid. The lack of lawful authority for the sale directly contributed to the violation of the petitioners' rights, as it undermined the principles of transparency and accountability that are essential in public governance. Conclusion The Supreme Court ruled in favor of the petitioners, declaring that their fundamental rights had been infringed due to the unlawful sale of shares of SLICL. The Court ordered the return of the shares to the Secretary to the Treasury and mandated the return of the purchase price to the purchasing companies. The judgment emphasized the need for adherence to legal and constitutional standards in the privatization of public assets, reinforcing the importance of public interest in such transactions. The Court's decision serves as a significant precedent in upholding the rule of law and protecting citizens' rights against arbitrary executive actions. Keywords fundamental rights — public interest — administrative actions — share sale — quasi contract — legal ownership — Treasury Bonds — corporate governance — auditor removal — profit entitlement
Counsel: S.A. Parathalingam, PC with Nihal Fernando, PC, N.R. Surendran, Rajendra Jayasinghe, Harshula Seneviratne, Ms. Nishendram on the instructions of Upendra Gunasekera for the 28th and 29th Respondents in SC FR 117/2007; S.A. Parathalingam, PC with Nihal Fernando, PC, N.R. Surendran, Rajendra Jayasinghe, Harshula Seneviratne, Ms. Nishendram on the instructions of Upendra Gunasekera for the 3rd and 4th Respondents in SC FR 158/2007; Dr. Kanag-Isvaran, PC. With Lakshmanan Jayakumar and Niranjan Arulpragasam instructed by G.G. Arulpragasam for the 2nd Respondent in SC FR 117 and 27th Respondent in SC FR 158/2007; Viraj Dayaratne, PC. ASG. With Dr. Avanti Perera, DSG for the Hon. Attorney General.

Parties: Mohamed Thawfeek Mukthar and Others v. J.I. Ratnasiri
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; Jagath Balapatabendi, J.; P.A. Ratnayake, J.
Keywords: Land Acquisition — Compensation — Market Value — Act No. 15 of 1968 — Section 7 of the Land Acquisition Act — Burden of Proof — Condition of Land — Court of Appeal — Board of Review
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal concerning the compensation awarded for land acquired by the state under the Land Acquisition Act. The appellants, consisting of the heirs of the late Ummu Shifa Mukthar, contested the compensation awarded by the Divisional Secretary, J.I. Ratnasiri, which was based on the market value of the property as of the commencement of Act No. 15 of 1968. The property in question, located at Horadehipitiya Road, Kolonnawa, was acquired on 2 March 1979, and the appellants argued that the compensation should be calculated based on the condition of the land at the time of acquisition rather than the earlier date of 22 September 1968, when the Act came into effect. The Board of Review initially ruled in favor of the appellants, determining that the relevant date for valuation should be 24 April 1981, the date of the Section 7 notice under the Land Acquisition Act. However, the Court of Appeal later overturned this decision, affirming the original compensation amount of Rs. 350,000 based on the market value as of 22 September 1968. The appellants subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court, raising several legal questions regarding the interpretation of the law and the findings of fact made by the lower courts. Issues The Supreme Court was tasked with addressing the following issues: 1. Whether the Court of Appeal was wrong in rejecting the preliminary objection that no question of law had been disclosed, and that the matter was purely a question of fact regarding the state of the land in 1968 and 1981? 2. Did the Court of Appeal err by making a pronouncement that interfered with a finding of pure fact? 3. Was the Court of Appeal incorrect in concluding that the land had not undergone any change without contrary evidence being led? 4. Did the Court of Appeal misdirect itself regarding the burden of proof? Applicable Law Statutes: Land Acquisition Act; Act No. 15 of 1968 Cases Referred to: Collettes Ltd. v. Bank of Ceylon - [1982] 2 Sri L.R. 514; Mahawitharana v. Commissioner of Inland Revenue - (1962) 64 N.L.R. 217; Naidu and Co. v. The Commissioner of Income Tax - (1959) A.I.R. S.C. 359 Analysis Issue 1: Whether the Court of Appeal was wrong in rejecting the preliminary objection that no question of law had been disclosed? Rule applied: The determination of whether a question of law exists is a matter for the court to decide based on the facts presented. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the arguments presented by the appellants, who contended that the issues raised were purely factual and did not disclose any questions of law. However, the Court noted that the Board of Review had drawn inferences from primary facts without sufficient evidence to support its conclusions regarding the condition of the land in 1968. The Court of Appeal had correctly identified that the questions posed were indeed questions of law, as they involved the interpretation of statutory provisions and the applicability of legal principles to the facts at hand. The Court emphasized that the absence of evidence regarding the land's condition in 1968 rendered the Board's conclusions speculative and legally unsound. Finding: The Court concluded that the Court of Appeal was correct in rejecting the preliminary objection, affirming that the issues raised were questions of law. Issue 2: Did the Court of Appeal err by making a pronouncement that interfered with a finding of pure fact? Rule applied: The distinction between questions of law and questions of fact is critical in appellate review. Court's reasoning: The appellants argued that the Court of Appeal had overstepped its bounds by interfering with factual findings made by the Board of Review. However, the Supreme Court clarified that the Court of Appeal's role included reviewing the legal interpretations made by the Board. The Court found that the Board had not based its findings on a proper assessment of evidence but rather on inferences drawn from the statutory framework. The Court of Appeal's decision to overturn the Board's findings was thus justified, as it was based on a correct interpretation of the law. Finding: The Court determined that the Court of Appeal did not err in its judgment and that its interference was warranted given the circumstances. Issue 3: Was the Court of Appeal incorrect in concluding that the land had not undergone any change without contrary evidence? Rule applied: The burden of proof lies with the party asserting a change in condition. Court's reasoning: The appellants contended that the Court of Appeal had misdirected itself by concluding that the land had not changed without evidence to the contrary. The Supreme Court noted that the appellants had the burden to prove that the condition of the land had changed since 1968. The Court of Appeal had examined the relevant documents, including the Deed of Transfer, which indicated the land's condition at the time of purchase. The absence of evidence from the appellants regarding any changes in the land's condition supported the Court of Appeal's conclusion. Finding: The Court affirmed that the Court of Appeal's conclusion was correct, as the appellants failed to provide evidence of any changes. Issue 4: Did the Court of Appeal misdirect itself on the burden of proof? Rule applied: The burden of proof is governed by the Evidence Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The appellants argued that the burden of proof lay with the respondent to demonstrate that the land's condition had not changed. However, the Supreme Court clarified that the appellants, having asserted a change in condition, bore the burden of proving that assertion. The Court referenced the relevant sections of the Evidence Ordinance, which stipulate that the party asserting a fact must prove its existence. The Court concluded that the appellants had not met this burden, and thus the Court of Appeal's findings were justified. Finding: The Court determined that the Court of Appeal did not misdirect itself regarding the burden of proof. Interaction of Issues The issues presented were interrelated, as they all stemmed from the central question of how the condition of the land affected the determination of compensation. The Court's analysis highlighted the importance of evidence in establishing the factual basis for legal conclusions. The failure of the appellants to provide evidence regarding the land's condition in 1968 directly impacted the Court's findings on all issues. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the appeal, affirming the judgment of the Court of Appeal dated 11 March 2005. The Court found that the questions of law raised by the appellants were answered in the negative, and no costs were awarded. The decision underscored the necessity of evidence in legal proceedings and the proper interpretation of statutory provisions in determining compensation for land acquisition. Keywords Land Acquisition — Compensation — Market Value — Act No. 15 of 1968 — Section 7 of the Land Acquisition Act — Burden of Proof — Condition of Land — Court of Appeal — Board of Review
Counsel: Faiz Musthapha, PC, with Faizer Marker and Hussaain Ahamed for Appellants-Respondents-Appellants; M.N.B. Fernando, DSG, with Rajiv Gunatillake, SC, for Respondent-Appellant-Respondent

Parties: Peopleâ€™s Bank v. Gardiner Mawatha
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; N.G. Amaratunga, J; Saleem Marsoof, J.
